refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time targets, and (2) actually achieving measured performance within acceptance criteria. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral—it focuses on the functional obligation (enable execution) without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ CT_target under duty cycle and site conditions) remains a verification gate, not part of the FR itself. This separation allows DPs to address enablement independently of performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution's design, controls, and operational procedures permit the load→travel→dump cycle to be executed end-to-end without artificial constraints or blocking failures. Measure p95 cycle time under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions; confirm p95 ≤ CT_target. Verify that no design element (e.g., speed limiters, safety interlocks, operator interface latency) prevents achievement of the target when operated within design envelope.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable the load phase (bucket fill and engagement) to be executed within the cycle time budget.
CR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable the travel phase (loaded and unloaded transit) to be executed within the cycle time budget.
CR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable the dump phase (bucket positioning, material discharge, and bucket retraction) to be executed within the cycle time budget.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose cycle into sequential phases (load, travel, dump) with phase-specific timing budgets; allocate time margins to each phase; monitor phase completion via state machine; trigger next phase upon completion or timeout.
DP2: Implement parallel execution of non-blocking operations (e.g., travel during load preparation) with dependency constraints; use event-driven coordination to overlap phases where feasible.

DP3: Design adaptive cycle control that measures actual phase durations in real-time, compares against target, and adjusts subsequent phase parameters (speed, dwell, acceleration) to meet p95 constraint.
DP4: Establish fixed, pre-validated cycle sequence with hard-coded timing derived from worst-case site conditions; eliminate runtime variability through deterministic scheduling.

DP5: Implement hierarchical timing control: high-level cycle orchestrator manages phase sequencing; low-level controllers (load, travel, dump subsystems) execute within allocated time budgets with local feedback loops.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Implement hierarchical timing control with phase-level time budgets, state-machine orchestration, and local feedback loops. The cycle orchestrator (high-level) sequences load→travel→dump phases, allocates time budgets to each subsystem controller, monitors phase completion, and enforces p95 compliance via adaptive margin adjustment. Each subsystem (load, travel, dump) operates within its budget with local feedback to meet phase-exit criteria (e.g., load complete, position reached, dump empty) before timeout.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between cycle orchestrator and subsystem controllers if phase budgets are not clearly decoupled (risk: change to one subsystem timing forces orchestrator redesign).
Dependency on accurate measurement of actual phase durations; if sensors or timing infrastructure are unreliable, feedback loop becomes ineffective.
Assumption that subsystems can operate independently within budgets; if load phase duration depends on travel distance or dump phase depends on load mass, hidden couplings emerge.
Orchestrator must not assume fixed subsystem response times; if subsystem latency varies with operating conditions, p95 target may be missed.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is selected because it balances determinism (fixed phase sequence) with adaptability (local feedback and margin adjustment). Hierarchical structure isolates cycle timing logic from subsystem implementation details, supporting independent evolution. Coupling is manageable if phase boundaries are clearly defined and subsystem contracts (time budget, completion criteria) are explicit.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Subsystems (load, travel, dump) can reliably complete their assigned tasks within allocated time budgets under all site conditions (soil type, slope, load mass variation). RISK: If subsystem performance is highly variable, time budgets may be insufficient for p95 compliance; mitigation: conduct site trials to characterize variability and set conservative budgets.

ASSUMPTION: Cycle orchestrator can measure or infer phase completion with sufficient accuracy (e.g., via position sensors, load sensors, pressure switches). RISK: Sensor failures or drift cause incorrect phase transitions; mitigation: implement sensor redundancy and self-check logic.

ASSUMPTION: Adaptive margin adjustment (DP5 feedback mechanism) can respond fast enough to correct drift without destabilizing the cycle. RISK: Slow feedback loops or aggressive adjustments cause oscillation or missed targets; mitigation: validate control loop stability during design phase.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined cycle time target is achievable given the machine's physical capabilities (engine power, hydraulic flow, bucket capacity). RISK: If target is unrealistic, no DP can satisfy it; mitigation: validate target against machine specifications and site conditions early.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan does not impose additional constraints (e.g., mandatory dwell times, reduced speed zones) that conflict with cycle time target. RISK: Safety requirements may force longer cycle times; mitigation: align safety plan with cycle design during requirements phase.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) the definition of 'cycle completion' for each phase (e.g., load complete = bucket full or time limit reached; travel complete = position within tolerance; dump complete = bucket empty or time limit reached); (2) the acceptable variability in cycle time (e.g., p95 ≤ CT_target implies 5% of cycles may exceed target—confirm this is acceptable); (3) the site conditions under which p95 must be met (soil type, slope, load mass range, ambient temperature); (4) whether subsystems operate independently or have interdependencies (e.g., travel speed depends on load mass). This will enable tighter DP validation and reduce risk of misalignment between design intent and acceptance criteria.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Hierarchical timing control orchestrator and subsystem phase management, including cycle sequencing (load→travel→dump), time budget allocation, phase completion monitoring, p95 compliance enforcement, and local feedback loop coordination across load, travel, and dump subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives high-level mission directives (e.g., 'execute load-travel-dump cycle') that trigger the cycle orchestrator.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Reports phase progress, time budget consumption, phase-exit criteria status, and p95 compliance metrics to external monitoring systems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals safety violations if phase timeouts are exceeded or adaptive margin adjustments fail to maintain p95 compliance.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. The Vehicle module naturally owns the cycle orchestrator, subsystem controllers (load, travel, dump), phase sequencing logic, time budget allocation, and local feedback loops. All core timing control logic resides within vehicle autonomy. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces cross boundary, but this is expected and minimal.

Poor containment. NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not a subsystem. Placing hierarchical timing control here would require Vehicle to delegate orchestration upward, creating inverted control flow and tight coupling. Phase-level feedback and subsystem coordination would diffuse across Vehicle and NGHE Solution boundaries.

Very poor containment. Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop interface layer, not an autonomous control module. Placing cycle orchestration and phase timing here would require Vehicle to depend on Teleoperation for autonomous sequencing, creating severe coupling and architectural inversion. Local feedback loops and subsystem state machines belong in Vehicle autonomy.

Very poor containment. Safety Services is a cross-cutting concern module for safety validation and alerts, not a timing orchestrator. Placing hierarchical timing control here would scatter phase sequencing and budget allocation across Safety Services and Vehicle, violating separation of concerns. Safety Services should monitor and alert on timing violations, not orchestrate them.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural and correct boundary for DP5 because it owns the autonomous control logic, subsystem controllers, and real-time state machines required to execute hierarchical timing control. The cycle orchestrator is a core Vehicle autonomy function that sequences phases, allocates budgets, and enforces compliance. Subsystem controllers (load, travel, dump) are Vehicle subsystems that operate within allocated budgets and report phase-exit criteria. Local feedback loops are internal Vehicle control loops. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are minimal and expected.
containment_risks: Risk: If subsystem controllers (load, travel, dump) are implemented as separate modules outside Vehicle, the phase-level feedback loops and budget enforcement will diffuse across module boundaries, requiring Vehicle to coordinate external subsystems. Mitigation: Ensure all subsystem controllers are owned by Vehicle or tightly coupled via well-defined internal interfaces.

Risk: If p95 compliance monitoring and adaptive margin adjustment are delegated to Safety Services, timing control logic will split between Vehicle and Safety Services. Mitigation: Keep adaptive margin adjustment in Vehicle; Safety Services monitors and alerts on violations only.

Risk: If Teleoperation can override or inject phase timing directives, the hierarchical timing model becomes non-deterministic. Mitigation: Teleoperation sends high-level Tasking directives only; Vehicle orchestrator maintains exclusive control of phase sequencing and timing.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The Vehicle module owns the cycle orchestrator and subsystem controllers, enabling direct measurement of phase completion times, time budget consumption, and p95 compliance against CT_target. Telemetry interface provides observable metrics (phase progress, budget status, p95 compliance) for external validation. Phase-exit criteria (load complete, position reached, dump empty) are measurable state transitions within Vehicle autonomy. Acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly verifiable via instrumentation of the Vehicle module's cycle orchestrator.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR allocation to Vehicle is unambiguous: the Vehicle module is responsible for executing the load→travel→dump cycle within CT_target. The DP (hierarchical timing control with phase budgets, state-machine orchestration, local feedback loops) is a cohesive design solution that maps entirely to Vehicle's autonomous control domain. No functional ambiguity exists regarding which module executes the cycle or enforces timing compliance.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 is a logically sound and complete solution to the FR. Hierarchical timing control with phase-level budgets directly addresses cycle time predictability. State-machine orchestration ensures deterministic phase sequencing (load→travel→dump). Local feedback loops enable real-time phase-exit criterion validation. Adaptive margin adjustment provides a mechanism to maintain p95 compliance under variability. The DP is implementable, testable, and traceable to the acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ CT_target). No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Subsystem controllers (load, travel, dump) must be owned by Vehicle or tightly coupled via well-defined internal interfaces. If these controllers are implemented as external modules (e.g., separate Subsystem A, Subsystem B), phase-level feedback loops and budget enforcement will diffuse across module boundaries, requiring Vehicle to coordinate external state machines. Mitigation: Confirm subsystem controller ownership within Vehicle module scope or establish explicit internal interface contracts (e.g., phase-exit criteria signals, budget consumption reports) with clear latency and reliability guarantees.

Risk: Adaptive margin adjustment logic must remain within Vehicle orchestrator. If p95 compliance monitoring and margin adjustment are delegated to Safety Services, timing control logic will split between Vehicle (phase sequencing) and Safety Services (margin adjustment), creating a distributed control loop with potential race conditions or delayed responses. Mitigation: Keep adaptive margin adjustment as a Vehicle-owned function; Safety Services monitors and alerts on violations only (passive monitoring, not active control).

Risk: Teleoperation must not inject or override phase timing directives. If Teleoperation can modify time budgets or force phase transitions, the hierarchical timing model becomes non-deterministic and p95 compliance becomes unverifiable. Mitigation: Enforce architectural boundary: Teleoperation sends high-level Tasking directives (e.g., 'execute cycle') only; Vehicle orchestrator maintains exclusive control of phase sequencing, budget allocation, and timing enforcement.

Risk: SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces cross the Vehicle boundary. While this is expected and minimal (containment score 0.92), ensure these interfaces do not introduce feedback loops that could delay phase transitions or corrupt state machine logic. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert and Telemetry as asynchronous, non-blocking interfaces; Vehicle orchestrator must not wait for acknowledgment before proceeding with phase transitions.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment within Vehicle module (score 0.92). The cycle orchestrator, subsystem controllers, phase sequencing logic, time budget allocation, and local feedback loops are all naturally owned by Vehicle autonomy. The DP is cohesive and does not scatter across multiple module boundaries. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are minimal and appropriate for a top-level autonomous control module. No architectural inversion or inverted control flow detected. The Vehicle module is the correct and only appropriate boundary for DP5 realization.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly document subsystem controller ownership: Confirm that load, travel, and dump subsystem controllers are owned by Vehicle or define clear internal interface contracts (phase-exit criteria signals, budget consumption reports, timeout handling) if they are separate modules.

Clarify adaptive margin adjustment ownership: Confirm that adaptive margin adjustment logic resides exclusively within Vehicle orchestrator. Define the interface between Vehicle and Safety Services as monitoring-only (Safety Services observes p95 metrics and alerts on violations, but does not modify timing parameters).

Define Teleoperation boundary: Document that Teleoperation sends high-level Tasking directives only and cannot override phase timing, budgets, or state transitions. Establish this as an architectural constraint.

Specify SafetyAlert and Telemetry interface semantics: Define these as asynchronous, non-blocking interfaces. Confirm that Vehicle orchestrator does not wait for SafetyAlert acknowledgment or Telemetry delivery before proceeding with phase transitions.

Add phase-exit criteria specification: Document measurable phase-exit criteria (e.g., 'load complete' = bucket full sensor + load arm at rest, 'position reached' = GPS within 2m + velocity < 0.1 m/s, 'dump empty' = tilt sensor angle > 45° + material flow sensor inactive for 2s). These criteria are the basis for phase transition logic and p95 compliance verification.

Specify time budget allocation algorithm: Document how the cycle orchestrator allocates CT_target across load, travel, and dump phases (e.g., fixed percentages, dynamic allocation based on site conditions, adaptive reallocation based on phase history). This is critical for reproducibility and p95 compliance.

Define p95 compliance enforcement mechanism: Specify how adaptive margin adjustment works (e.g., reduce phase budgets by X% if p95 is trending above CT_target, increase by Y% if trending below). Include convergence criteria and safety bounds (e.g., minimum phase budget floor to prevent unsafe operation).

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is clear, the DP is logically sound and implementable, and the IM (Vehicle module) is the correct architectural boundary with excellent containment (0.92). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that critical sub-level design decisions remain unspecified: (1) subsystem controller ownership and internal interface contracts, (2) adaptive margin adjustment algorithm and ownership, (3) phase-exit criteria specification, (4) time budget allocation algorithm, and (5) p95 compliance enforcement mechanism. These are not defects in the current level but rather dependencies that must be resolved at the next decomposition level to ensure implementability and verifiability. Given high safety criticality, deriving explicit sub-FRs for these concerns is essential before detailed design proceeds. Stopping here would leave critical architectural and control-logic decisions deferred, increasing integration risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Allocate cycle time budget across load, travel, and dump phases based on site conditions and historical phase performance to enable deterministic phase sequencing and p95 compliance.
FR5.2: Orchestrate phase transitions (load→travel→dump) using measurable phase-exit criteria and enforce phase-level time budgets to ensure cycle completion within CT_target.
FR5.3: Monitor phase execution time against allocated budgets and adjust future phase budgets adaptively to maintain p95 compliance under variability.

FR5.4: Coordinate subsystem controllers (load, travel, dump) via well-defined internal interfaces (phase-exit criteria signals, budget consumption reports, timeout handling) to enable hierarchical timing control.
FR5.5: Enforce architectural boundary with Teleoperation to prevent override of phase timing, budgets, or state transitions; accept only high-level Tasking directives.
FR5.6: Provide asynchronous, non-blocking SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces to external modules without blocking phase transitions or corrupting state machine logic.

revise.why: No revisions required at current level. FR5, DP5, and Vehicle IM all pass gates. Proceed to next-level decomposition to resolve sub-level design decisions.

assumptions: Subsystem controllers (load, travel, dump) are owned by Vehicle module or tightly coupled via explicit internal interface contracts with guaranteed latency and reliability.

Adaptive margin adjustment logic resides exclusively within Vehicle orchestrator; Safety Services operates in monitoring-only mode (observes metrics, alerts on violations, does not modify timing parameters).

Phase-exit criteria are measurable state transitions (e.g., load complete = bucket full + arm at rest; position reached = GPS within tolerance + velocity < threshold; dump empty = tilt angle > threshold + material flow inactive).
Teleoperation sends high-level Tasking directives only and cannot override phase timing, budgets, or state transitions.
SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces are asynchronous and non-blocking; Vehicle orchestrator does not wait for acknowledgment before proceeding with phase transitions.
Time budget allocation algorithm is deterministic and reproducible (e.g., fixed percentages, dynamic allocation based on site conditions, or adaptive reallocation based on phase history).

p95 compliance is measured and enforced via instrumentation of the Vehicle module's cycle orchestrator; acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly verifiable.
open_risks: Subsystem controller ownership ambiguity: If load, travel, dump controllers are external modules, phase-level feedback loops and budget enforcement will diffuse across module boundaries, creating coordination complexity and potential race conditions. Mitigation: Confirm ownership within Vehicle or establish explicit internal interface contracts with latency/reliability guarantees.

Adaptive margin adjustment distributed control: If p95 compliance monitoring and margin adjustment are delegated to Safety Services, timing control logic splits between Vehicle (phase sequencing) and Safety Services (margin adjustment), creating potential race conditions or delayed responses. Mitigation: Keep adaptive margin adjustment within Vehicle; Safety Services monitors only.

Teleoperation override risk: If Teleoperation can modify time budgets or force phase transitions, hierarchical timing model becomes non-deterministic and p95 compliance becomes unverifiable. Mitigation: Enforce architectural boundary; Teleoperation sends high-level directives only.

SafetyAlert/Telemetry blocking risk: If Vehicle orchestrator waits for SafetyAlert acknowledgment or Telemetry delivery before proceeding, phase transitions may be delayed, violating time budgets. Mitigation: Define interfaces as asynchronous and non-blocking.

Phase-exit criteria specification gap: Measurable phase-exit criteria are not yet formally specified. Ambiguity in criteria (e.g., 'load complete' definition) will propagate to state machine logic and p95 verification. Mitigation: Derive FR5.2 to specify phase-exit criteria formally.

Time budget allocation algorithm unspecified: Algorithm for allocating CT_target across phases is not yet defined. Without this, phase sequencing logic cannot be implemented or verified. Mitigation: Derive FR5.1 to specify allocation algorithm.

p95 compliance enforcement mechanism unspecified: Adaptive margin adjustment algorithm (how to adjust budgets based on p95 trending) is not yet defined. Without this, p95 compliance cannot be actively maintained. Mitigation: Derive FR5.3 to specify enforcement mechanism.
evidence_needed: Subsystem controller architecture diagram showing ownership boundaries and internal interface contracts (phase-exit criteria signals, budget consumption reports, timeout handling).
Formal specification of phase-exit criteria for load, travel, and dump phases (e.g., sensor thresholds, state conditions, timing constraints).
Time budget allocation algorithm specification (e.g., fixed percentages, dynamic allocation rules, adaptive reallocation logic) with justification for chosen approach.
Adaptive margin adjustment algorithm specification (e.g., feedback control law, convergence criteria, safety bounds on minimum phase budgets).
Teleoperation interface specification confirming that only high-level Tasking directives are accepted and phase timing/budgets cannot be overridden.
SafetyAlert and Telemetry interface specification confirming asynchronous, non-blocking semantics and that Vehicle orchestrator does not wait for acknowledgment.
State machine diagram for cycle orchestrator showing phase transitions, phase-exit criteria, budget enforcement, and adaptive margin adjustment logic.
Instrumentation and telemetry specification for p95 compliance verification (e.g., phase completion time measurements, budget consumption tracking, p95 calculation method).
Integration test plan for subsystem controller coordination, phase transition timing, and p95 compliance under agreed duty cycle and site conditions.
